perturbation.original_text: Â§ 3 Control Panel (1) After successfully completing the registration process for participation in the Program, the Affiliate will be sent a user ID and a password to access to the "Affiliate Control Panel". With the user ID and password, the "Affiliate Control Panel" provides the Affiliate access, via a secure online connection, to a secure area on element 5's Web server so that the Affiliate may, at any time, view the number of software sales the Affiliate has referred, as well as change other settings.
perturbation.changed_text: Â§ 3 Control Panel (1) After completing the registration process, the Affiliate may be sent a user ID and a password to potentially access the "Affiliate Control Panel". With the user ID and password, the "Affiliate Control Panel" might provide the Affiliate access, possibly via a secure online connection, to an area on element 5's Web server so that the Affiliate may, if possible, view the number of software sales the Affiliate has referred, as well as change other settings.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using words like 'may be sent,' 'potentially access,' 'might provide,' and 'if possible.' These terms weaken the certainty of access and functionality of the Affiliate Control Panel, making the obligations of Element 5 vague and uncertain.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 12 - Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject
perturbation.law_explanation: The GDPR requires that data controllers provide clear and transparent information about how personal data is processed and accessed. By making access to sales data and settings discretionary and uncertain ('may', 'potentially', 'if possible'), the modified text contradicts the GDPR's mandate for transparent and readily available access to personal data-related information, including sales data related to the affiliate's performance, creating a vague system of accessibility. The GDPR is applicable because element 5 GmbH is located in Cologne, Germany.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: f differences in the implementation and application of Directive 95/46/EC. (10) In order to ensure a consistent and high level of protection of natural persons and to remove the obstacles to flows of personal data within the Union, the level of protection of the rights and freedoms of natural persons with regard to the processing of such data should be equivalent in all Member States. Consistent and homogenous application of the rules for the protection of the fundamental rights and freedoms of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data should be ensured throughout the Union. Regarding the processing of personal data for compliance with a legal obligation, for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller, Member States should be allowed to maintain or introduce national provisions to further specify the application of the rules of this Regulation. In conjunction with the general and horizontal law on data protection implementing Directive 95/46/EC, Member States have several sector-specific laws in areas that need more specific provisions. This Regulation also provides a margin of manoeuvre for Member States to specify its rules, including for the processing of special categories of personal data (âsensitive dataâ). To that extent, this Regulation does not exclude Member State law that sets out the circumstances for specific processing situations, including determining more precisely the conditions under which the processing of personal data is lawful. (11) Effective protection of personal data throughout the Union requires the strengthening and setting out in detail of the rights of data subjects and the obligations of those who process and determine the processing of personal data, as well as equivalent powers for monitoring and ensuring compliance with the rules for the protection of personal data and equivalent sanctions for infringements in the Member States. (12) Article 16(2) TFEU mandates the European Parliament and the Council to lay down the rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and the rules relating to the free movement of personal data. (13) In order to ensure a consistent level of protection for natural persons throughout the Union and to prevent divergences hampering the free movement of personal data within the internal market, a Regulation is necessary to provide legal certainty and transparency for economic operators, including micro, small and medium-sized enterprises, and to provide natural persons in all Member States with the same level of legally enforceable rights and obligations and responsibilities for controllers and processors, to ensure consistent monitoring of the processing of personal data, and equivalent sanctions in all Member States as well as effective cooperation between the supervisory authorities of different Member States. The proper functioning of the internal market requires that the free movement of personal data within the Union is not restricted or prohibited for reasons connected with the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data. To take account of the specific situation of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises, this Regulation includes a derogation for organisations with fewer than 250 employees with regard to record-keeping. In addition, the Union institutions and bodies, and Member States and their supervisory authorities, are encouraged to take account of the specific needs of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises in the application of this Regulation. The notion of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises should draw from Article 2 of the Annex to Commission Recommendation 2003/361/EC ( 5 ) . (14) The protection afforded by this Regulation should apply to natural persons, whatever their nationality or place of residence, in relation to the processing of their personal data.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the affiliate's access to their data and the control panel's functionality, using terms like 'may be sent,' 'potentially access,' 'might provide,' and 'if possible.' This directly weakens the certainty of Element 5's obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 highlights the need for 'legal certainty and transparency' regarding data processing. The original text provided a clear expectation of access. The altered text undermines this transparency, making it uncertain whether the affiliate can access their sales data, which contradicts the GDPR's emphasis on providing clear information about data processing and access.

perturbation.original_text: Â§ 5 Guarantee by the Party to the Agreement for its Web site (1) The Affiliate shall be responsible for the entire content of its Web site. The Affiliate guarantees in particular that (i) It will integrate advertising Material according to the provisions and instructions of the Software Publisher into that Affiliate's Web site insofar as said Material was transferred to the Affiliate; (ii) The material used the Affiliate's Web site will not contain any representations of violence, sexually related contents or discriminatory statements or representations with regard to race, sex, religion, nationality, handicap, sexual preference and orientation, or age, nor will said Material be unlawful in any way;
perturbation.changed_text: Â§ 5 Guarantee by the Party to the Agreement for its Web site (1) The Affiliate is generally responsible for the content of its Web site. The Affiliate aims to ensure that (i) It attempts to integrate advertising Material according to the general provisions and guidance of the Software Publisher; (ii) The material used on the Affiliate's Web site should ideally avoid representations of violence, sexually suggestive contents or unfairly discriminatory statements regarding race, sex, religion, nationality, handicap, sexual preference and orientation, or age, and should generally be lawful;
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be responsible' to 'is generally responsible', 'guarantees' to 'aims to ensure,' 'will integrate' to 'attempts to integrate,' 'will not contain' to 'should ideally avoid,' and 'will said Material be unlawful' to 'should generally be lawful,' the modified text introduces significant ambiguity. It weakens the strict obligation of ensuring lawful and non-discriminatory content to a more subjective and less enforceable standard, potentially conflicting with laws against discrimination and the promotion of harmful content.
perturbation.law_explanation: The German General Equal Treatment Act (AGG) prohibits discrimination based on race, ethnic origin, gender, religion or belief, disability, age, or sexual orientation. By weakening the guarantee that the Affiliate's website 'will not contain any representations of violence, sexually related contents or discriminatory statements,' the modified text creates an ambiguity that could allow for content that violates the AGG. The phrases 'generally responsible', 'aims to ensure', and 'should ideally avoid' do not provide the level of assurance required by the AGG to prevent discrimination and the promotion of unlawful content, thus creating ambiguity and potential non-compliance. The AGG applies because element 5 GmbH is based in Cologne, Germany.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity by weakening the affiliate's responsibility for its website content. The original text uses terms like 'shall be responsible' and 'guarantees,' creating a strict obligation. The changed text uses softer terms like 'generally responsible,' 'aims to ensure,' and 'should ideally avoid,' diminishing the enforceability of the content standards. The LLM explanation is that the AGG (German General Equal Treatment Act) prohibits discrimination, and weakening the guarantee against discriminatory content creates ambiguity that could allow violations of the AGG. The modified phrases do not provide adequate assurance to prevent discrimination or unlawful content. Snippet 2 is not usable, and Snippet 1 is missing. However, the original text makes it very clear what the obligations were of the affiliate and makes clear guarantees of such. The changed text does not, and this weakening could lead to discrimination violations, in violation of the AGG.

perturbation.original_text: Â§ 2 Changes to these provisions element 5 RESERVES THE RIGHT TO MODIFY AND TO SUPPLEMENT THESE GENERAL TERMS AND CONDITIONS AT ANY TIME. THE CURRENT VERSION WILL BE MAINTAINED FOR VIEWING AS A HYPERLINK ON element 5's WEB SITE IN THE CONTROL PANEL. MOREOVER, element 5 WILL ANNOUNNOUNCE ANY CHANGES TO THESE TERMS VIA E- MAIL. WHITHIN 14 DAYS OF THE ANNOUNCEMENT OF CHANGED PARTICIPATION TERMS, AFFILIATE AND SOFTWARE PUBLISHER MAY OBJECT TO SUCH CHANGES. IF NO OBJECTION IS RECEIVED DURING THIS TIME, THE PARTICIPANT IS DEEMED TO HAVE ACCEPTED THE CHANGES AND THEY WILL BECOME PART OF THE EXISTING RELATIONS UNDER THE AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: Â§ 2 Changes to these provisions element 5 MAY MODIFY AND SUPPLEMENT THESE GENERAL TERMS AND CONDITIONS. THE CURRENT VERSION MIGHT BE MAINTAINED FOR VIEWING AS A HYPERLINK ON element 5's WEB SITE. MOREOVER, element 5 MIGHT ANNOUNCE ANY CHANGES TO THESE TERMS VIA E-MAIL. AFFILIATE AND SOFTWARE PUBLISHER MAY OR MAY NOT OBJECT TO SUCH CHANGES.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'reserves the right to modify and supplement' with 'may modify and supplement,' 'will be maintained' with 'might be maintained,' and 'will announce' with 'might announce,' the text introduces ambiguity regarding the obligation of Element 5 to provide notice of changes to the terms. Further, by removing the 14-day period for objection and the automatic acceptance clause, the text introduces ambiguity as to whether the changes are actually binding.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under German law, changes to standard business terms (Allgemeine GeschÃĪftsbedingungen, AGB) require the explicit or implicit agreement of the contracting parties. By removing the clause that deems the participant to have accepted the changes if no objection is received within 14 days, and by making the announcement and availability of the changes uncertain, the amended text creates a situation where it is unclear whether the affiliate or software publisher has actually agreed to the modified terms. This contradicts the principle of requiring agreement for changes to contractual terms, as outlined implicitly in the German Civil Code (BGB) and specifically regarding AGB. The BGB is applicable because element 5 GmbH is located in Cologne, Germany. The deletion of the 14-day period is relevant because without that period, there is no defined time to accept the new contract.

Executory contracts and unexpired leases (a) Except as provided in sections 765 and 766 of this title and in subsections (b), (c), and (d) of this section, the trustee, subject to the court's approval, may assume or reject any executory contract or unexpired lease of the debtor. (b)(1) If there has been a default in an executory contract or unexpired lease of the debtor, the trustee may not assume such contract or lease unless, at the time of assumption of such contract or lease, the trustee- (A) cures, or provides adequate assurance that the trustee will promptly cure, such default other than a default that is a breach of a provision relating to the satisfaction of any provision (other than a penalty rate or penalty provision) relating to a default arising from any failure to perform nonmonetary obligations under an unexpired lease of real property, if it is impossible for the trustee to cure such default by performing nonmonetary acts at and after the time of assumption, except that if such default arises from a failure to operate in accordance with a nonresidential real property lease, then such default shall be cured by performance at and after the time of assumption in accordance with such lease, and pecuniary losses resulting from such default shall be compensated in accordance with the provisions of this paragraph; (B) compensates, or provides adequate assurance that the trustee will promptly compensate, a party other than the debtor to such contract or lease, for any actual pecuniary loss to such party resulting from such default; and (C) provides adequate assurance of future performance under such contract or lease. (2) Paragraph (1) of this subsection does not apply to a default that is a breach of a provision relating to- (A) the insolvency or financial condition of the debtor at any time before the closing of the case; (B) the commencement of a case under this title; (C) the appointment of or taking possession by a trustee in a case under this title or a custodian before such commencement; or (D) the satisfaction of any penalty rate or penalty provision relating to a default arising from any failure by the debtor to perform nonmonetary obligations under the executory contract or unexpired lease. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding Element 5's obligation to notify affiliates/publishers of changes to terms, and removes the clause for automatic acceptance after 14 days. This directly undermines the requirement for agreement on modified terms. While the provided snippets are not directly applicable, the Law Explanation states that German law (BGB) requires explicit or implicit agreement for changes to standard business terms. The removal of the 14-day acceptance period and the weakening of the notification obligation contradict this principle. The changes introduce ambiguity about whether the affiliate or publisher has actually agreed to the new terms.
